### Post by irv on 2007-10-23
> **irv said:**
> So far I have completed ten complete installs of Ubuntu 7.10 and have done one of the edubuntu 7.10.  I also have done 2 upgrades. One to my server and one to my laptop. Nine of the ten complete installs went great, but I am having some issues with the last one I did. It was done on an HP laptop. Here is what happen.
I booted from the live CD and started the install. Everything went well but it gave me a warning about not able to install security updates. I saw that it was not connecting to  my wireless network. After correcting the problem I continued with the install. At that point it continued but did not install the security updates. After rebooting everything seem to work well, but when my friend took his laptop home it would not connect to the Internet. 
After he brought it back to me, I checked it on my wire from my router and found it would not work with DHCP on the wire connection. After taking it back to his house I found the same problem there. I had the ISP check his connection and**[COLOR="Red"] I am planning to boot with the Live CD again this morning to see if the Live CD will see his Internet.[/COLOR]** If it does I thought about doing a re-install. If anyone knows of something else I could try please let me know? 
The only advice I would offer to anyone doing a complete new install is to check the Internet connection before starting the install. If I would have done this, I believe things might have gone much smoother on the last install I did. The only difference between the first nine installs was the fact that they were done on desktops and were on the wire not the wireless. After booting from the Live CD they all connected to the Internet before starting the install.

Irv

In the above post I said I was going to boot with the Live CD again to see if I could get on the Internet. Well I could so I did the Install again and everything works fine. I also setup a new router and hook the laptop up to it and it sees the wire and the wireless connection just fine.
Irv

### Post by wolfjb on 2007-10-23
laptop experience: 
* Synaptics touchpad horizontal scroll no longer works, no errors in /var/log/Xorg.0.log

Desktop experience:
* won't boot 2.6.22-14-generic nor 2.6.22-14-386, kernel crash during boot when trying to find my HD. oddly doesn't stop the boot process at that point, but will eventually drop to a shell since it can't find UUID=whatever. I can mount /dev/hda2 which is where my root partition is located. changing the bootarg root= from UUID (don't understand what that is about or what advantage it gives me, wish the install would STOP doing that - I know what /dev/hda2 is but I have no idea what UUID=c095f436-34f5-4532-80d1-9fe2867511ea is) to /dev/hda2 doesn't work either

* no window manager ever starts, have to manually start metacity (booted with 2.6.20)

* nvidia driver no longer works (again, booted with 2.6.20 since 2.6.22 doesn't boot) but used to before the upgrade. I have enabled the driver with the restricted driver dialog, but it never works. I have to use the nv driver which doesn't give me any 3D acceleration.

### Post by thecarpy on 2007-10-23
feisy to gusty
Well, I had no X after the first reboot, I had no safe mode graphics just a nice login: prompt ... so I logged in and started gdm (sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start), still nothing well, no, he cannot find the kernel module for my new kernel, so I installed open source nv driver, rebooted (to be on the safe side) and X started in safe mode  I had no x, because I am a nightmare with graphics card drivers, so I guess it is my fault ...
 ... OK, in safe mode I clicked too fast (so it is my fault again) and had beautiful 1980's graphics - you know the res even my mobile phone can take 640x480.... ;-) there I try to access the "proprietary driver control panel-thingy" and get the message that drives me nuts ever since they implemented that panel - you need the "restricted blahblah" for this thingy to work !!! HELLLO, you silly panel HAVE admin privs, ask me if you may go and get it! I get it for the silly panel, get the nvidia dirver, reboot, tell him to enable it.log out log in and it works, yahoo!!! then I enable beryl eeerhm compiz-fusion, sorry and it all works. Now I have a second remark for the compiz-fusion stuff, just like the restricted driver manager, I believe it would be handy, if you get the compizfusion-settings-manager installed from with-in the appearance panel, as soon as you click on the Visualization tab and choose advanced vizualition, you should be asked if you want to configure advanced settings ..... and offer you to go get the settings manager .... I think this really loooks cool, works well  - Nice work guiyz!

### Post by myke on 2007-10-23
SOMEONE PLEASE EXPLAIN THIS TO ME ... Here's my problem:

[B][I]A while back I posted about my upgrade problem with Gutsy.  Everything upgraded fine but all videos, no matter what format (.avi .mpeg .wma, etc.) would cause whatever program I used to open them to freeze immediately (kaffeine, totem, mplayer).   Could NOT figure out what was causing it.

### Post by Alan Percy on 2007-10-23
I installed 7.10 to dual boot with Xandros 4.0. Live CD worked great to partition HD and install was smooth. However, on reboot, GRUB did not show the Xandros partition. Fixed by using live GpartEd at boot and now use LILO as boot manager as it correctly recognises all partitions and OS.

Printer was a challenge, but resolved. Still can't connect to internet. The Xandros Network Connection wizard is far easier, more effective and intuitive to ex Microsoft exiles.:confused:

Gnome Eye much better for seeing photographs on the system than is the Xandros equivalent. Stoked to have Gimp 2.4!:)

### Post by froy02 on 2007-10-23
It tuned out very good.  The installation and upgrade.  There were some issues but I have a laptop also  a0nd  and thus I can surf the net for the answers specially that Installation for dual boot was a little tricky... "GRUB" and all that.

I installed Ubuntu on the 2nd hardrive but when I removed it my win XP would not boot without the 2nd hd.  That does happen with windows as long as you specify which is the boot drive in the bios. I had to fix the MBR then re-install Ubuntu.  I could have just as easily recovered by recopying my back up clone but I had to find something about "grub" which windows does not use.

I hope grub can be modified so that it would boot on that drive where it is installed if it can not find the 2nd hard drive and/or partition.

The greatest tool is the synaptics package manager.  I have forgotten the DOS method and I am not familiar with the linux commands.  SPM is just great.

A UBuntu convert,
Froy

### Post by CaesarLike on 2007-10-23
Hi all,

I am quite new to Linux, and Ubuntu, only been using for a few months.  I upgraded to the new 7.10 without any problems at all.

I did the upgrade using the Alternate ISO.  7.04 recognised it immediately.  Upgrading from the ISO went without even the slightest problem, including downloading an extra 190 Mb the installer said I required.

The PC is an old 1.7 Celeron, with around 750 Mb ram, and an old Nvidea Gforce 5200 FX PCI graphic card.  I use this PC for torrents.

The only real problem I have experienced with 7.10 has been burning CD's with the old CD burner in the PC.  7.10 does not like this burner for some reason ( 7.04 had no issues with it).  I assume the CD burner is a bit too old now.  I put in a newer LG DVD/CD combo burner, and no more CD burning problems.

Apart from this slight CD issue, everything I have tried with 7.10 has worked without problems.  10/10 to the Ubuntu team for a first rate Linux Distro.  Now its time to put Ubuntu on my P4 3.0 machine, and try out the new visual effects. :biggrin:

### Post by cessna on 2007-10-24
I'm really frustrated, because I cannot get Gutsy to install at all.

I've purchased a new system (ASUS M2A-VM HDMI motherboard with integrated ATI X-1250 graphics chipset, AMD AM2 4800+ CPU, 2gb RAM, 320Gb SATA drive, etc...) specifically to run Gutsy on, but it won't install.

It gets as far as trying to start KDM, but fails back to a text screen where the last message is "...Running rc.local.....[OK]". It sits there forever and gets no further (i.e. I've left it there for an hour, still no progress!). I can use ALT-F1 to select one of the other text screens, which is running a shell which is working, so the machine hasn't frozen - but the install has stopped.

However, installing Fedora 7 from DVD works flawlessly. It only drives the graphics in VESA mode, but everything else is just fine, so I suspect that it's something to do with the kernel supplied with Gutsy not liking the ATI graphics card at all.

The Feisty (7.04) install DVD also boots fine - I stopped the install at the point of partitioning the disks, as I already had Fedora 7 installed by that stage.

So, something is broken in Gutsy - not happy!
:(

### Post by thomaswp on 2007-10-24
I did an upgrade from Feisty to Gutsy (Dell Inspiron 1300)

Everything seemed OK but then I realised my WiFi (Broadcom 43xx) was down.  Gutsy said something about proprietary drivers but whilst they loaded they didn't work.

I tried alot to get the wifi working.  My old favourite "[HOWTO: Broadcom 4318 Wireless Cards]("http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=197102")" didn't do the trick this time with ndiswrapper or native drivers.  My eth1 disappeared and was replaced with eth1:avahi in the status bar.  I realised eventually I could overtype the avahi bit to get the eth1 status but this was not obvious.  

After much searching and hair pulling, I found " [HOWTO: Broadcom 43xx based wireless cards the EASY way]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=405990")." and this got my WiFi working again.  Unfortunately this fix led to my ubuntu hanging on boot (3.5 squares in to the classy splash screen).  I could get in in safe mode...  Help from someone through the forum led me to remove the **splash** parameter in grub to see what was going on.  Most mysteriously the boot does NOT hang when the splash screen is disabled.

I am currently working out how to look at the boot log (if there is one) to see what is going on.

### Post by Wingcommander on 2007-10-24
Upgraded (rather rashly) from 7.04.
I have never, ever, ever upgraded within days of something coming out before. God alone knows what possessed me to do it this time. It's not been very pleasant, but I do blame myself for not waiting.

Problems

1. Swiftweasel
Switfweasel stopped working. Seemed to hang after booting. If you just booted it up and did nothing, within 2 mins it was frozen. Had to use a Windows machine to search the web. Switched to firefox ok. I only used Swiftweasel coz I couldn't get Flash to work on Firefox. Flash seemed to work fine though now.

2. Slow web access
I switched to Firefox and that at least loaded, but web pages were very slow when using URL's, indicating a possible DNS issue. Direct IP stuff (putty etc) was fine. Fixed by using opendns settings instead of your dhcp and disabling ipv6 (supplied by someone on these boards - about:config, network.dns.diableipv6=true)

3. Sound
My microphone no longer works with Skype.
Ekiga softphone has very poor, crackly sound.

4. Printer
My USB Epson Photo R300 no longer prints, despite being detected ok. Tried all the suggestions on this board that I can find, Still dead.

Good stuff

Flash player seems to play without any audio/video sync issues.


Advice from my experience? I'd leave it a while unless this is your spare computer.

------
Linux? It's certainly no worse than Windows Vista...

---

### Post by johnny9794 on 2007-10-24
Yes, My install worked flawlessly, I had to install with the alternate cd, reboot to recovery mode and type in dkpg-reconfigure xserver-xorg in the cmd and set the driver to VESA for my MSI NX6600LE Graphics Card"7.10 and earlier I cannot use the live-cd, I have to use the alternate cd.". nv and nvidia was making my screen all scrambled in colored blocks, How a member helped me and as well, helped my self, found the conclusion of using vesa driver.

I use gparted alot on an old system where is had no problems running gparted on my old pc, until i got this new pc that would not work with any debian based live-cd's nor would gparted live-cd's, until gparted came out with a new option's for different gfx cards. Well i ran gparted live-cd-0.3.4.8 normally and it crashed and somewhere it said vesa driver failed and in gparted main menu options i remembered seeing a option called force vesa driver, so i tried it and boom gparted works wonders, so I made a forum about this no-devices-found and mention that i got gparted working on a driver called vesa. I got a great reply and solution thanx to tseliot.

The Forum:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=531247](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=531247)

Solution Below:

> **tseliot said:**
> If you tried the livecd and you was not able to install Ubuntu even when using the Safe Mode:

1) Install Ubuntu using the alternate installation CD.

2) After the installation the xserver will crash

3) You will be able to boot in RECOVERY MODE from the GRUB Menu (select it using your keyboard) almost as soon as you turn on your computer (it will take you to the command line).

Then you will need to type:
```
dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg
```

and select the "vesa" driver. Press ENTER whenever you don't know the answer to a question.

4) Type:
```
reboot
```

On next reboot the Xserver should work fine


NOTE: if the problem is not solved try using "vga" instead of "vesa" and try again.

If nothing works and the Xserver just crashes, please post the output of the following command:
```
lspci
```



Did it worked flawlessly ? Answer: Yes
Did you got problems ? Answer: somewhat not that minor.
Did you manage to solve them ? Answer: Yes
if yes how ? Ubuntu Member and myself.

### Post by jimbreslauer on 2007-10-24

Overall, Gutsy comes across as a more solid & polished product than Feisty. I'm quite happy with it.

### Post by wislon on 2007-10-24
I upgraded from (fully updated) Feisty off the alternate CD (no access to high speed internet here), and it took about 40 odd minutes, and other than having to answer a couple of questions about some customised config files, it went off without a hitch.

After I rebooted, the X server wouldn't start properly, it showed the "X" cursor and I started to panic. Tho I think that had something to do with my dual monitor setup. 

However an ancient looking vesa-looking config UI eventually came up and worked well enough for me to tell it I had a GeForce 6-series card (had to squint a bit to see it), and after I set that it was able to load up in 640x480 mode (ugh) on one monitor. 

At that point it then came up and told me that I was using a restricted driver (the nvidia one), and after I told it to enable it, it went off and did some more thinking, and voila, all of a sudden I could use a single monitor in 2048x1024. 

A bit more fiddling finally got my dual-monitors both working, tho I battled a bit with the nvidia control panel. Eventually I gave up and copied a backup of my previously working dual-monitor xorg.conf file, hit ctrl+alt+backspace and was in business.

Later last night, it popped up and told me it had found about 200MB of updates, so I told it to go and do it and went to bed. When I logged on this afternoon, it told me it had found a number of other updates (mostly software I was using, no real OS based stuff), which I installed as well.

Everything else is working just peachy so far. My vmware is complaining I need to renconfigure it (surprise!), and when I hit the "Log off/switch user etc." icon in the top left of the taskbar, everything freezes until I hit ctrl+alt+backspace, but I'm sure I'll find solutions to all of these.

Personally I am very impressed with the whole process. I know other ppl here have had problems, and I'll honestly say I am glad I am not one of them!

---

### Post by eyemou on 2007-10-24
For Kubuntu:

My upgrade from Edgy to Feisty was 100% painless.

Feisty to Gutsy was another story, entirely.

After updating my repo's and downloading the updates, the installation phase hung pretty early on. After a bit, I got annoyed and killed it. From there, after and apt-get update / upgrade (and maybe a 'dpkg --configure -a' and an 'apt-get -f install'), most of it installed, until it hung up on another package.

I was stuck for a bit, as I was unable to install/uninstall/upgrade anything via apt-get.

If memory serves, I purged the problem package with aptitude, reinstalled it, and went back to apt-get update/upgrade/ -f install. Aside from having to manually install a handful of specific packages, it was more or less smooth sailing from there.

Post-install, the only thing I had to do was to reinstall the Firefox form widgets, gtk-qt-engine (that disappeared somewhere during the upgrade), and mess with my fonts a bit. Oh, and now that I think of it, I also had a weird 'unknown mimetype' issue, and an issue with menus in kcontrol. I forget what the cause of the problem was exactly, but I was able to solve it (and jotted down the fix at home, in case I see someone with a similar issue).

All seems to be running well, however, I'm noticing a bit more CPU usage, and some slower performance in Firefox. However, other apps (kcontrol, konsole, kwrite, sysguard spring to mind) seem to open and perform a lot faster.

### Post by Nonno Bassotto on 2007-10-24
After the upgrade I had the following problems (I'm considering a fresh install):

1. The resolution for my Intel video card was not correct anymore. Solution: install 915resolution
2. I lost my compiz settings. Solution: recreate them with patience.
3. Got the white border bug in Compiz. Solution: enlarge the shadows to 8.5 pixels.
4. Lost the sound preview. Solution: reinstall esound
5. Evince does not work inside Firefox anymore. No solution yet. A temporary solution is resizing Firefox to show the documents.
6. No sound in Skype after upgrade. Solution: reinstall esound and delete ~/.Skype
7. Login time increased from few seconds to 52 seconds. No solution yet.
8. The birthdays calendar in Evolution is now empty. No solution yet.
9. OpenOffice crashes when doing anything. Temporary workaround: remove openoffice.org-gtk
10. Jack doesn't start. No solution yet.

### Post by gabba on 2007-10-24
Hello, I upgraded a few days ago from Feisty to Gutsy...

On my end, at least the system is working, but I have many weird bugs:

- lost boot splash. Note that I had upgraded from feisty to ubuntu-studio feisty, then uninstalled ubuntu-studio, reinstalled ubuntu-desktop package, and upgraded. But still, I would have expected that the upgrade would install its own boot splash. Worse, after 1h30 of searching forums and wikis, I still don't find a way of reinstalling the default boot splash.

- I get a weird usb error, that seems to be about a usb port where nothing is plugged in (address 2)???

- Starting time from entering my password at the gdm screen to having the desktop fully loaded is slow, really bad compared to feisty.

- Network-manager applet is sluggish to respond to commands, while under feisty it attempted connecting as soon as I clicked on a network.

- Default java was set to GNU java instead of Sun Java, I was wondering why Eclipse was running so buggily...

Some deceptions:

- Network-manager STILL doesn't have an easy option to disable automatically connecting to networks you clicked before. It's been what, 6 months? Meanwhile, people that don't know how to fiddle with gnome settings are forced to connect illegally to their neighbors' network, if they clicked it by accident. Way to go Gnome and Ubuntu!

- Deskbar applet was more useful when you could type right in the bar, now you have to open a window first.

- Desktop effects are buggy, I had to change some settings in xorg.conf to have my title bars; furthermore there are still some weird drawing bugs. Pretty standard Nvidia card here. Was it really time to include this in the distro?

- Did I mention everything is slower? Feisty was on par with windows XP, now gutsy feels slower again.

- On an older machine where I'm maintaining a Xubuntu install for a friend, the slowing down from Xubuntu Feisty to Xubuntu Gutsy was just unbelievable. (Thanks God, the upgrade caused less bugs.) Who said that linux is useful to save old computers, again?

- Wanting to be more active with the Ubuntu community, I started searching launchpad for bugs a bit more. What amazed me is that some pretty serious bugs were reported, the worse being some bugs in Xubuntu Feisty, but nothing was done to fix them until the next release. Now, it's nice to have releases often, but why not fix the bugs in the current version first? Otherwise you upgrade to solve some problems, and lo and behold, you have acquired brand new bugs to replace your old ones.

- Browsing launchpad also showed that some very popular user requests such as file preview in file opening dialogs are being repeatedly ignored, and this since 3 releases at least. Windows has had it for ages, and it's very useful for all users. What am I supposed to answer to my computer-illiterate friends which I am initiating to Linux: "Oh, you won't get an image preview for when you want to email a photo to your friends, because the wise decision-makers at Ubuntu decided it was bad for you?"

---

> I already had 7.04 installed and I downloaded the ISO.
Tried to use the live cd but it seems that it won't upgrade my current installation and my only option is to reformat my ubuntu drive and start from scratch if I used the cd.
Am I wrong?

Anyway, started my current ubuntu and used 
gksu "update-manager -c"


... :( ......
.... and now I have to wait for 100+ megs of stuff to download. Why the F couldn't it just get that stuff off the 600+ MB cd that I already downloaded?


::update::
downloads complete. Restart. Check System->About Ubuntu.
Still says I'm using 7.04
using gksu "update-manager -c" again

Ok, now it says it's upgrading me to 7.10

.... :( ... The waiting begins :(

::update::

AND WHAT A WAIT IT IS!!!
1 hour and 13 minutes.
It only took 45 minutes for me to download the .iso from mininova!

WHY DOESN'T IT JUST GET THESE THINGS OFF THE CD THAT I ALREADY DOWNLOADED !!!!!
why why why why why why why why why why why why why why why why why why why why

*!*.... well, it's midnight.

**** this. 
I've got work in the morning.
You must
1. Have the CD repo in your software sources (System -> Administration -> Software Sources)

2. USE THE ALTERNATE CD!!!

It will not use the LiveCD because that is a preinstalled environment, it doesn't contain any raw .deb files for upgrading.

### Post by adza on 2007-10-24
Well i thought it's about time i posted about my experience as there seems to be many people posting their negative expereinces. I am a linux rookie (this is my third release - so probably not nub anymore), but still fairly new to the OS and learning everyday. I did a fresh install onto a whole disk about 4 days ago now and while i initially thought the install process took longer than 7.04 & 6.06 it still worked flawlessy with all my periphials (HP Printer, Apple 20" Cinema Dispaly, ipods, usb hub) being recognised OOB and the screen resolution being detected (1680 * 1050). Installing the Nvidia restricted drivers was a simple task and again, worked flawlessly - now my desktop cube and effects are also humming. From there it was time to begin re-installing all my tweaks, beginning with flash for firefox. While i admit i'm using the 'non-free' plugin, i am stoked that there now exists a 64 bit version and it installed simply. 
Skype still seems to be an issue in 64 bit, however i found a great project from [Cappy called getlibs]("http://http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=474790&highlight=getlibs+project") which solves 32 bit dependency issues. What a great project this is, and for what it's worth, massive kudos goes to Cappy for this one ***kudos*** :) So i had the basics running, now it's time to setup my development environment beginning with the LAMP server. I installed these through synaptic and it was again extremely simple to do, the only 'challenge' was remembering to create the symbolic link into my /var/www/ directory for the phpmyadmin pages (hehe for lazy admin in dev environment). Then i was up and running without issue. Admittedly, i have left the hardest part to last as i have to try to install java and phpeclipse tonight but i'm sure i will be able to sort this out as well... 
Lastly, and this is what is going to kill my windows install completely (hehe), the ETQW linux client has been released and now this game is happily running on my gutsy system, this was the easiest install of the lot to be honest - to those that say linux isn't for gamers, it appears that ID & Splash Damage have found the way forward on this front. 
Anyway, this is why i've voted for the install - worked flawlessy option, because it did :)

---

### Post by TheThirdBardo on 2007-10-24
Just finished the upgrade (including fixing the most obvious problems), and it went like this:

I stepped into this bug: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/adept/+bug/154493](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/adept/+bug/154493) (similar to [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/update-manager/+bug/107188](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/update-manager/+bug/107188))
With some help from this forum I finally skipped the graphical update-manager and ran:
```
apt-get dist-upgrade
apt-get autoremove
apt-get install kubuntu-desktop
```

Next problem was after the reboot, the window-rewriting was very sluggish. I found out that a process called Xgl was eating a lot of CPU, so after investigating it on the forum, I found a suggestion to remove the xserver-xgl package. Then it went fine again.

Other than that I have not found any obvious problems, but they may show up over time. (Oh, yeah: lirc didn't work straight on, but it never does, so it's no news (it needs recompilation on kernel upgrade).)

---

[   4.848000] hub 1-1.2:1.0: connect-debounce failed, port 2 disabled 


[   4.848000] hub 1-1.2:1.0: cannot disable port 2 (err = -71)


[   4.848000] hub 1-1.2:1.0: hub_port_status failed (err = -71)


any help greatly appreciated, as i really would like to avoid having to do a re-install, and ive definately not got any usb devices plugged in.

Appart from this, i really liked gutsy so far.  desktop effects work off the bat with no errors (another minor problem i had in feisty).  love the crypto option in the installer.

good work, and thanks.

### Post by orthorim on 2007-10-24
Worked but more problems that the Feisty install.

- Graphics card ATI X1600 mobility, was not working out of the box, some command line voodoo needed to make it really work. I was stuck with no 3D acceleration and no option to switch to my native screen resolution of 1680x1050 until I used the command line tool (search for X1600 in the forums)
- Wireless was dog slow after install, lots of ubuntu forum searching revealed that IPv6 has to be turned off by changing several config files per hand. 

Both of these things worked flawlessly in Feisty - e.g. Feisty managed to automatically install and configure both wireless and the ATI card with no problems.

- Suspend mode crashes the machine. Don't think this worked in Feisty either.

Can't recommend Gutsy on laptops.

I like glitz as much as anyone but if basic things don't work it doesn't do anything for me.

### Post by tjbk_tjb on 2007-10-24
Yesterday, I decided to upgrade my 64-bit Kubuntu Feisty Fawn to Gutsy Gibbon. So, I went and followed the [instructions on the wiki]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/GutsyUpgrades#head-3692aaaed415e3427f54ec62dd8659474516b525"). After some initial difficulty, the update program came up and started upgrading. 3 hours later, it said it had to restart some things. I noticed that it said gdm on the list. Since I didn't have gdm, I exchanged that for kdm and pressed continue. My X server preceded to restart. But the upgrade program did not. So I went on the forum for help. I read recommendations of the use of 'dpkg --configure -a' and 'apt-get upgrade.' I tried that a few times, along with the use of Aptitude to resolve problems. So, I think I'm all ready to go. Terminals say "7.10" and KDE has a shiny new theme.
Then I try to run Thunderbird. It doesn't start. I try it in the terminal. I get:
```
/usr/lib/thunderbird/thunderbird-bin: symbol lookup error: /usr/lib/libgdk-x11-2.0.so.0: undefined symbol: g_once_init_enter_impl
```
Later, I tried Firefox when I was having trouble with Flash (which was easily resolved by installing a package). Same message. Firefox was working, but only with kgtk-wrapper, which I installed manually. But that didn't help with GIMP, Abiword, or Thunderbird, all of which returned the same error. I tried reinstalling libgtk2.0-0 (package responsible for /usr/lib/libgdk-x11-2.0.so.0), but that doesn't help.

So, can anyone help me with this?

### Post by bergersau on 2007-10-25
Overall very smooth upgrade.

BUT

Upgrade broke my sound. Module wasn't being loaded for my onboard sound AD1988b.
Fixed by running 
```
sudo aptitude install linux-ubuntu-modules-$(uname -r)

```

Also had to edit my xorg.conf for the mouse buttons after nvidia-glx-config re-wrote the file when re-configuring the desktop reslution.

Minor things but potential showstoppers for new linux users.

On the plus side the Nvidia-new drivers in the restricted drivers manager finally work for me and compiz fusion is working outta the box. Previously I had to manually install the Nvidia drivers direct from the Nvidia site - and re-install after every kernal update.

Overall Score A-   Good job devs keep it up.

### Post by rogowar on 2007-10-25
I've had a few different versions of Ubuntu, Kubuntu, Mepis and whatever running on an ancient dell I was trying to get a bit more life out of.  I just did a fresh install of gutsy Xubuntu, mostly tying to eke out as much performance as possible.  Well, it was flawless, automatically configuring previous pain points.  I was interested to see the Restricted Driver Manager, which I did try for my old nVidia card.  The excellent thing is that the supported driver actually performed much better, configured the full 1600x1200 resolution, and made me happy.  

Another pain reliever was installing my Brother MFC Printer/Scanner/Fax.  The printer installed from the Add Printer wizard, automatically detecting the ip socket of the printers NIC.  The scanner software installed exactly as the instructions on the Brother site described.  I was able to get full duplex printing from various applications, and have scanned in some text and images over the network.  I think I'm going to leave the new laptop alone and play with my dinosaur.

### Post by jasonsweet on 2007-10-25
It went pretty easily for me.  I have some problems.

1.  Firefox kept freezing up on pages with Flash.  I installed the latest 64bit flash ( I use the x64 software) and it fixed the problem, immediately.  

2.  At first, the boot up and the opening of programs seemed to be just fine.  Then I configured my network setting and it slowed to a crawl.:confused:  It took 60-90 seconds for programs to open.  I back tracked my steps, and noticed I left the wired LAN card Roaming Mode unchecked.  For some reason, that slowed the computer down tremendously.  I ticked the roaming mode, and it returned to normal.:)

3.  Wireless seems a little slow, but it never had been great with Feisty.  I have a Dell 640m laptop with a Boradcom 43xx wireless driver.  Works, but not a strong of a signal as in Windows and I have hard time picking weak wireless signals that I can pick up in Windows..  In fact, if Ubuntu fixed this one problem, I would dump Windows all together.

### Post by venik212 on 2007-10-25
Here is my experience in upgrading from Kubuntu 7.04 to 7.10:
I followed the upgrading instructions, which did NOT work.  However, I persisted, and after repeating the process a few times, insisting that I want to Fetch Updates, it did, eventually, start upgrading.  It worked without a hitch once it started.  I did that on two different machines, with the same exact experience.

Things that did not work in 7.04 and still do not work:
1) Scanner: Canoscan N650U, which works perfectly on the same computer running XP, does not work with any of the programs I tried (Kooka, Xsane).
2) EasyCam or EasyCam2 start but do nothing at all, and do not proceed to install any drivers.
3) OpenOffice is still NOT compatible with MS OFFICE-- WORD and POWERPOINT documents cannot be trusted to be converted properly.  (I know this is not a Linux issue, but it is a problem for me, forcing me to continue to use MS Windows).

In short, Windows will be here for a long time to come, I am sad to say.

---

### Post by dawynn on 2007-10-25
I upgraded, but had to keep the Feisty kernel for awhile because of problems with the Gutsy kernel recognizing ide drives (as opposed to ata).  See Bug 133666.  Note that the bug includes a patch that, to my knowledge, has not been applied yet.  But, Bug 151013 documents a work-around that, with a little tweaking to convert from Mac to PC, did help the kernel work.  After that, I was finally able to clear all Feisty packages from my system.

I had a GUI at that point, but my nvidia drivers still did not seem to like to use glx.  Which left me without my game software.  That was finally fixed this week after some playing with nvidia-xconfig.

So, two major issues -- a kernel that doesn't like ide drives, and nvidia drivers that require tweaking for each computer.  Everything else seems to be working.

---

### Post by jay767 on 2007-10-25
I voted for "worked flawlessly."

Computer: Asus K8N, AMD 1.8GHz 64-bit, 250 GB SATA, 80 GB IDE, ATI Radeon 9600, 100% Pure Linux (32-bit)

I'll say right off the bat that my reason for updating was motivated purely by frustration. My left mouse button is wearing out and while jumping through Nautilus I often found myself in a strange directory. Since I prefer list views, about 66% of my screen was the "last modified" column. Being tired and cranky I decided to set it to update to 7.10 and relax instead of messing with patches that may or may not work

Having had some bad experiences with ATI video cards and updated drivers in the past (mind you, with Fedora) I turned off my restricted video driver first and made sure my important files were on my usb key. Paranoid, I know.

I have to admit that I was a little nervous seeing all the applications and lib-this and lib-thats being removed and feared that I'd have to rebuild my favorite applications but surprise surprise everything worked right after the update. I didn't even have to edit a xorg.conf file (if Ubuntu has it by that name; haven't needed to look yet) at all.

The only hiccup I encountered was when I tried to get some of the advanced desktop features going. I hadn't made an effort to install xserver-xgl or any of the Compiz packages and had a strange "composite extension not found" error box in Preferences->Appearance until I got everything set up. No biggie.

EDIT
Well, fair-sized biggie. I actually got rid of xgl and compiz because it slowed everything down by an extreme amount. Not really a problem with the update though just a package problem.

---

### Post by JohnCraickkciarc on 2007-10-25
> **venik212 said:**
> Here is my experience in upgrading from Kubuntu 7.04 to 7.10:
I followed the upgrading instructions, which did NOT work.  However, I persisted, and after repeating the process a few times, insisting that I want to Fetch Updates, it did, eventually, start upgrading.  It worked without a hitch once it started.  I did that on two different machines, with the same exact experience.

Things that did not work in 7.04 and still do not work:
1) Scanner: Canoscan N650U, which works perfectly on the same computer running XP, does not work with any of the programs I tried (Kooka, Xsane).
2) EasyCam or EasyCam2 start but do nothing at all, and do not proceed to install any drivers.
3) OpenOffice is still NOT compatible with MS OFFICE-- WORD and POWERPOINT documents cannot be trusted to be converted properly.  (I know this is not a Linux issue, but it is a problem for me, forcing me to continue to use MS Windows).

In short, Windows will be here for a long time to come, I am sad to say.
I attempted an on-line upgrade to a fully up to date kubuntu 7.04 installation. The new packages download took ~ 24 hours and then the upgrade wizard hung up permanently part way through its operation. I then used a series of apt-get console commands to complete the upgrade. After all that I found that  the 7.10 version had borked the wireless LAN connection that had previously operated fine under 7.04. On Kubuntu, the official networking tools have never worked for me & I have to use start up scripts to make it all work. Even with these scripts, on my system (Athlon XP 2200) the Ubuntu 7.10, kernel 2.6.22-14-386 won't run networking at all and I have to use the alternative 2.6.20-16 version. The 2.6.22 kernel also won't run the fuse drivers that give r/w access to my NTFS drives.

Overall, it's a fine learning experience, if you like that sort of thing, but I'm mighty unimpressed with this latest version.

I also have a 610u canon scanner which the **previous** kubuntu upgrade broke. I posted a fix for that, which works for some people, at 

[http://www.ubuntugeek.com/fixing-a-scanner-broken-by-the-feisty-upgrade.html](http://www.ubuntugeek.com/fixing-a-scanner-broken-by-the-feisty-upgrade.html)

- JohnC

### Post by The Chef on 2007-10-25
Spent the week after upgrading from Feisty to Gutsy looking at a blue screen after boot.  Traced the problem to 7.10 bluetooth services hanging when a flash memory card reader was attached to a USB port (NB: no real bluetooth devices present).  It even prevents the 7.10 live disk from loading when it is plugged in.  See [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=591135](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=591135) for solution that fixed the upgrade.

### Post by wormbo on 2007-10-25
Fresh install on my old test box.

Pentium 4
1GB RAM
Nvidia FX5500
2 IDE HDD's with NTFS
1 SATA HDD with NTFS / ext3 / swap

WORKED:
All Harddrive detected perfectly. NTFS readable and mounted off the bat.

Restricted drivers installed flawlessly and automatically.

DIDDNT WORK:
I was unable to resolve names in any web browsers...
I was able to update packages fine.
I was able to resolve names using PING.
I was able to browse to sites by IPv4 address

HOW I FIXED IT:
I had to disable native IPv6 support completely
Disabled IPv6 dns lookups in firefox ( in about:config )


This was very annoying and had me running around on the internet for a fix for at least 3 hours straight.

I am running on a standard ADSL modem/router, which apparently hates IPv6.
I tried different cabling, statically assigning DNS, and IP address.
Tried renewing IP via DHCP...

>: (

---

### Post by pssl220 on 2007-10-25
I have a Dell Inspiron 9200 with ATI graphics card.

I was  using 7.04 with no problems then did a clean install to 7.10

So far I have:
    1)  bug # 121653 listed in the release notes - suspend/resume does not work
    2) bug # 150930 also listed in the release notes -  blank boot splash and VERY long boot up time

I was able to work around the second bug  by using the work around suggested in the bug description. 

The first one I can't work around and the bug fix is only "wish list"!

Both of these are very serious. I am disappointed Ubuntu allowed 7.10 to be released knowing that there were these major regressions. Just when people are looking to get away from Vista, people will try Ubuntu go running back to MS.  The 7.10 release should have been kept a beta until these major problems were fixed. 

Blaming ATI or Nvida does not help the large percentage of people stuck with these components on their computers.

### Post by psych-major on 2007-10-25
Well, I've been using Ubuntu on my laptop for about a year now and have been VERY happy. 32 bit was flwaless to install and interact with on a daily basis, but 64 bit was a little flaky, specifically concerning my Atheros wifi card. And don't even get me started on the lack of 64 bit java, but that's not Ubuntu's fault. 
It was my intention to revert back to 32 bit, but I thought I would try a gutsy dist upgrade first just for fun. It ran and completed with only an error about being unable to stop "beagle-something.exe" but a pkill beagle took care of that. after the upgrade finished: no wifi and no sound. I manually compiled the madwifi driver, nada and didn't even bother with the sound, cuz WTF...the most user friendly distro in the world? the one my mother could install by herself? HARDLY!!!!

So fine, I wanted to go back to 32 bit anyhow...download...download..Comcast resets my bittorrent connection...download...burn cd...

Oh joy! 7.10 officially WILL NOT BOOT ON MY THINKPAD T60P!!!! It crashes on the launching of the x-server, and of course has no text installer by default...more downloading....

In the meantime, I'm posting this from the same Thinkpad running Slackware 12.0 which installed with no gotchas...no hitches...no special weird workarounds...just plain works, sort of like Ubuntu is supposed to...

Two notes, Slackware comes up with the vesa graphics driver. It's not pretty, but it works enough to allow one to fire up a web browser and grab the latest ati driver...then ./ati-driver thingy.run...yes..ok..yes..ok..no..ctl+alt+backspace and viola: 1680x1050.
Also, the ethernet connection worked out of the gate on Slack, but no wifi...paid a visit to the madwifi site, downloaded, unzipped, ./configure...make..make install..modprobe ath_pci...reboot....bada bing...wireless.

The thing that really torques me is that because this is my work laptop, I have to use Evolution for Exchange email. It's a pain in the butt to install on the Gnome-less Slack, but as it turns out, may be less work than getting the easiest distro in the world to even boot on my fairly plain laptop....

Booobuntu...you have totally lost my confidence in your efforts to become a household desktop for anyone who doesn't use a Dell.

---

### Post by irv on 2007-10-25
I have already posted on the thread on all the installed and upgrades I did (all 13 of them now), but would like to make one point I took note of.
When updating my laptop I keep an eye on the CPU temp and here is what I found. About six months ago my laptop over-heated and shut down. At that time I had a lot of things running in 4 workspaces and I believe I over worked my processor. Since then, every time I am running the processor for long periods of time I keep an eye on the temp. While doing the upgrade my temp would go from 140 degrees F. to 165 degrees. Normally it runs at about 115 to 140.  Right now as I type this it is running at 118.  I am not sure what the limit temp is in my CMOS.
I thought I would just pass this observation on to those who might be interested.
Irv

---

### Post by herbster on 2007-10-25
Two installs:

1) Home pc:
[LIST]
[*]P4 EE3.4ghz
[*]1.2gb ram
[*]BFG GeForce 7600GS
[*]M-Audio Revolution 7.1
[*]2 x WD 320gb SATA + WD 500gb SATA as external (in enclosure)
[*]Dual monitors (LCD+CRT)
[/LIST]

Installed, copied over /home from Feisty and boom, everything as it was. (It's amazing how my xorg.conf and .asoundrc, two files barely a few kb, are responsible for pretty much all of my customized video and sound). No problems whatsoever, all works.

2) Sister's laptop which has Vista:
[LIST]
[*]Celeron 1.73ghz
[*]882 megs ram
[*]80gb hd
[*]ATI Radeon Express 200M
[*]onboard sound
[/LIST]

Everything works perfectly. I installed XGL and busted out Compiz, my goodness I could not believe how smooth and flawless it was. I still remember my ATI card on my home PC earlier this year and my headaches getting into an XGL session and how poorly it performed, before I even ran Beryl-- the progress in this respect that the linux geniuses out there have made is commendable. Also, wireless worked flawlessly right off the bat, I was easily able to see all the wireless networks and connect to the one I had been using in Vista. Perfect all-around.

I am thoroughly impressed with Gutsy, and my sister is now learning to use Ubuntu. I showed her how to do everything she was doing in Vista, on Ubuntu.

The only thing I can think of is random freezing. It's rare, but it does happen and it's frustrating to have so many things open I'm working on only to have it lock up.

### Post by pichita on 2007-10-25
I'm running Feisty 7.04 with all latest updates.

I've downloaded the Gutsy 7.10 alternate CD and procceeded to upgrade as per the instructions:

Insert the CD and run:
sudo /cdrom/cdromupgrade


This worked fine (it opened the Upgrade manager). The upgrade started and passed steps 1 and 2 (preparing the upgrade, and modifying the software channels),

But then at step 3 (fetching the upgrades) the upgrade manager entered into a loop. Here fetching the upgrade means copying them from the CD, as the noise of the CD driver was clearly telling me that this is what was going on. The manager went through the list of 984 packages to upgrade 3 times, and after that failed due to a checksum mismatch for two packages!

### Post by adza on 2007-10-25
> 
Well i thought it's about time i posted about my experience as there seems to be many people posting their negative expereinces. I am a linux rookie (this is my third release - so probably not nub anymore), but still fairly new to the OS and learning everyday. I did a fresh install onto a whole disk about 4 days ago now and while i initially thought the install process took longer than 7.04 & 6.06 it still worked flawlessy with all my periphials (HP Printer, Apple 20" Cinema Dispaly, ipods, usb hub) being recognised OOB and the screen resolution being detected (1680 * 1050). Installing the Nvidia restricted drivers was a simple task and again, worked flawlessly - now my desktop cube and effects are also humming. From there it was time to begin re-installing all my tweaks, beginning with flash for firefox. While i admit i'm using the 'non-free' plugin, i am stoked that there now exists a 64 bit version and it installed simply. 
Skype still seems to be an issue in 64 bit, however i found a great project from Cappy called getlibs which solves 32 bit dependency issues. What a great project this is, and for what it's worth, massive kudos goes to Cappy for this one ***kudos***  So i had the basics running, now it's time to setup my development environment beginning with the LAMP server. I installed these through synaptic and it was again extremely simple to do, the only 'challenge' was remembering to create the symbolic link into my /var/www/ directory for the phpmyadmin pages (hehe for lazy admin in dev environment). Then i was up and running without issue. Admittedly, i have left the hardest part to last as i have to try to install java and phpeclipse tonight but i'm sure i will be able to sort this out as well... 
Lastly, and this is what is going to kill my windows install completely (hehe), the ETQW linux client has been released and now this game is happily running on my gutsy system, this was the easiest install of the lot to be honest - to those that say linux isn't for gamers, it appears that ID & Splash Damage have found the way forward on this front. 
Anyway, this is why i've voted for the install - worked flawlessy option, because it did 

Well, i've installed eclipse and my php dev environment is again purring... from what i thought to be the biggest challenge that would face me in this install, again this was suprisingly easy! Simply installed eclipse from synaptic, upon running initially it couldn't find the jvm, so i installed one!! haha, this was easily fixed through the ubuntu-restricted-packages install, then everything worked sweetly.. In addition to this i also installed the media player and other restricted codecs at a single click (just try to run a .wmv file in totem and it will find the right packages for you to install), again another breezy install. To top all this off, i now have gdesklets re-installed as the bug that was causing the deamon to fail has been fixed and a .deb package has been posted on launchpad taking all the stress out for us! I have been without my eye candy desklets since dapper (didn't do edgy - was still too nub) as the workarounds were deemed (by me) not worth the effort in feisty.. so i would like to upgrade my vote from a 9 to a 10, fantastic effort for the gutsy dev team, what a great distro...

---

### Post by linux4me on 2007-10-25
I used the update manager to upgrade. 

Problems I had:
[LIST=1]
[*]no sound after the upgrade
[*]Intel 536 modem didn't work after the upgrade
[/LIST]

I got the sound working using info from one of the posts here, and following the steps in [this part of the wiki]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/DialupModemHowto/Intel536EP") for 7.04 got my modem working in Gutsy.

I could never get suspend to work correctly for this machine in Fiesty despite all kinds of effort spent tweaking settings in /etc/default/acpi-support. It worked about 90% of the time, but that other 10% was a killer; it would suspend, but when I tried to resume it wouldn't have any video output. Only a Ctrl-Alt-Del would get it back. 

In Gutsy, suspend has been working flawlessly without any tweaks. That alone will make the time it took to upgrade worthwhile.

I like the flashy new GUI features, too.

Thank you Ubuntu!

### Post by dmsa31 on 2007-10-25
I'm using Xubuntu on my Compaq X1000 laptop (Radeon 9200). I upgraded from Feisty using the Update Manager. Things went smoothly for the most part, except for a couple of things:

1. I have an external monitor hooked up to my laptop via VGA. With Feisty, when I started X with the monitor plugged in it would automatically blank the laptop's LCD. Now, it starts with both monitors on, and I have to disable the internal one using xrandr. Until the internal LCD is disabled, the external monitor is limited to the internal one's resolution (1280x800).

2. It won't seem to recognize my custom modeline in xorg.conf, instead using the mode that it got from autodetecting the monitor. The problem is that this mode is a bit so-so.

3. I'm getting random, though rare, crashes in X. I can still move the mouse around, but nothing is responsive. This has never happened before in feisty, and is so far the biggest show-stopper for me. I'm assuming it has something to do with the new graphics.

Overall I'm pretty pleased with Gutsy, but I wish that there was a way to completely disable the Screens and Graphics tool. It's completely useless to me in my situation; it doesn't even let me run my internal and external monitor at the same time, which I'm assuming it was designed to do.

### Post by grEnAlEins on 2007-10-25
Worked, with a few issues.

1) Internet did not work on campus.  This was the fault of my school, not Ubuntu or myself.  This was easily fixed by finding a spot that I was able to connect from and downloading a java plugin so that I could log into Cisco Clean Access Agent via java applet.  EASILY RESOLVED

2) Wireless did not work out of the box.  Quite fix, downloaded restricted driver.  EASILY RESOLVED

3) No DVD play.  This is a work in progress.  I did it on the last 2 releases, just forgot how.  Nothing that google should not be able to fix.  SHOULD BE FAIRLY EASY TO RESOLVE

4) When I plug in my headphones, the speakers do not mute. IDK what to do.  I CANNOT RESOLVE THIS ON MY OWN...

5) Cannot wake my computer up from sleep.  I do not use sleep other than to test if it worked, so no big deal.  I DO NOT CARE ABOUT THIS ISSUE

All is well with the new release for me.  I run a Compaq Presario C501NR (same as a C500, but somehow different).

### Post by Dinadan on 2007-10-26
**[ Voted "Upgrade - worked but had a few things to solve".**

Ubuntu 7.10 is an excellent release.  Although I had some "things to resolve", the whole process was much simpler and cleaner than my most recent forced re-install of windows XP.

I have three computers and have successfully installed Ubuntu 7.10 on two of them with minimal problems[B]

**[SIZE="3"]Main HTPC (Dual Boot with WinXP):**[/SIZE][/B]

    * First try failed with repository errors
    * Deleted several seldom used programs installed using Automatix
    * Uninstalled Automatix and edited the repositories config file to # out the offending repository
    * After that, Ubuntu 7.10 installed with no problem.
    * I was still able to use the nVidia control panel that remained from the original Automatix nVidia Driver install
          o  this is one of the things I did not uninstall in the steps above.

Upgraded the monitor on this system today to a V7 Budget 22'"  Wide-screen LCD Monitor:

    * I was able to set up native resolution and other settings perfectly through the nVidia control panel.

I have a separate /Home partition - all of my existing applications from before the upgrade (nVidia excepted) continue to work with no problem or need to make adjustments

**[SIZE="3"][SIZE="3"]Secondary PC:  Dell 3000 (A Difficult Dual Boot with WinXP):[/SIZE][/SIZE]**
Took a little fussing around but update was successful:

    * I have an nVidia Geforce 5200 PCI graphics card that the standard installer could not identify. The Live disk would even not boot
    * Needed to start in Safe Graphics Mode - I would recommend that to anyone who has ever had video problems problems to use this.
    * Ubuntu installed, but still seemed to have problems with the nVidia card and display settings.
    * Downloaded Alberto Millhone's Envy nVidia idriver nstaller, and used it to successfully update drivers -Everything then worked fine.
          o Great work Alberto!

I had moved the Acer 19" Monitor from the HTPC to his  Dell System today to replace a CRT behemoth that was failing:

    * I was able to set up native resolution and other settings perfectly through the nVidia control panel.


"**[SIZE="3"]Tertiary PC[/SIZE]**: (an ancient Gateway SB1000 Upgraded - nVidia 6200AGP, Memory, 250Gb Drive)[/SIZE][/B]
I was able to get the Ubuntu Live disk to start on this system, but installs fail at 35-60% with a disk read or I/O error (Errorno 5).
I verified the MD5 Checksum for the download ,and then the tried burning a couple of Ubuntu  7.10 ISO coasters at progressively lower burn rates, to no avail.  With a motherboard this old,
 I am not going to sweat it.  I plan to salvage the usable parts to build a new system with stuff from a Newegg wish List (when my savings reach the order trigger).

**[B][SIZE="5"][COLOR="Blue"]CONGRATULATIONS to the Ubuntu team -7.10 is a Great Accomplishment[/COLOR][/SIZE]**[/B]

Regards

     Garth

PS Your Forum Software is crap. : I have to deal width arcane format codes and a bizarre preview function  that does not seem to be useful.. 

It is the worst forum user input interface I have ever dealt with.

Why not move  to a useful  Forum Software Package

### Post by hamster_billy on 2007-10-26
Upgrade using the update manager.

The major thing which didn't work I expected and had worked out previously how to get round - all to do with Grub. I have a non-default options which need to be specified in order to boot, and of course when Grub updates the boot options everything gets screwed up:
"mem=2011M" must be specified to boot normally (though I can boot in safe mode /very/ slowly without this). This option became necessary when I upgraded from 1Gb to 2Gb ram; noone's been able to tell me why. I've now, with help from the forum, amended my Grub menu.lst so I shouldn't have problems again.

Compiz got a little screwed up, but eventually I found I just had to remove and reinstall the Compiz Config Manager. /Much/ easier than trying to get through the damage Feisty did to a working Compiz system.

gDesklets 0.35.3 didn't work, but all that was needed was to uninstall and reinstall.

A heck load of stuff has been put in the 'Other' section of the main menu. I haven't a clue what most of this stuff is, but it wasn't there beforehand and I don't think it should be there now. There's so much of it that it'll take a good hour or two to filter out the useful bits, and I haven't got round to doing it yet.

I'm very happy to have Bluetooth functioning better. In Feisty I'd miserably failed to make Bluetooth do anything useful at all, whereas in Gutsy I can at least transfer files to and from my mobile phone. I haven't tried using a headset yet.

Overall, the upgrade went more smoothly than that from Edgy to Feisty.

---

### Post by Detonate on 2007-10-26
Only problem I had was, getting my wireless working.  I was using NDISwrapper in Feisty, and when Gutsy upgraded it uses the new driver.  When it wrote to the /etc/network/interfaces file somehow the auto wlan0 entry was made twice and this prevented the wireless from connecting.  Once I figured that out everything else went smoothly.

### Post by keskew on 2007-10-26
I have been trying ubuntu for the last couple of months.  I installed it as a dual boot (without separate partitions) using wubi.  I really like ubuntu and decided to install a larger hard drive and do a true dual boot systems (Windows XP + ubuntu).  

Before the new hard drive arrived, I upgraded to gutsy in the wubi installed configuration.  The upgrade did not go well.  After the upgrade I received error messages that the HAL had failed.  I couldn't access the internet through wired or wireless connections.  I also couldn't install the restricted drivers to get my network cards working.

When the new hard drive arrived I installed it, used gparted to set up the partitions and then reinstalled Windows XP and ubuntu 7.10.  This installation of ubuntu was flawless.  The install went quickly.  As soon as I logged into ubuntu it informed me there were three restricted drivers I could install (2 for my network cards and 1 for my ATI graphics card).  As soon as I installed the restricted drivers I could access the internet through my wireless card.  When I installed feisty, it took me several days to get my wireless internet card and ATI graphics card to work properly.  So for a fresh install, I experienced a big improvement in this version.

I am not sure why, but compared to the feisty with wubi install, this configuration (gutsy with its own partition) boots much slower.

P.S. I am using an HP Pavilion ze2000 laptop.

### Post by sleepykit on 2007-10-26
Did a standard upgrade from Feisty to Gutsy using the Update Manager. Laptop is a Dell 1420n, nothing special about it. 

Below is my list of problems, solved and unsolved

1. Wouldn't boot into gnome at all (wrong driver listed in xorg.conf) - solved
2. Sound wouldn't work (alsamixer had everything muted) - solved 
3. Gnome takes over a minute to come up (rest of boot process seems fine) - not solved
4. Compiz Fusion does not work - solved - Chipset GM965 has been blacklisted in Compiz since it, EXA, Ubuntu, and Intel aren't playing well together
5. Jumpy network - not solved - I have no idea why but switching wireless networks now takes longer and my card seems to have more trouble connecting (network manager in use - WICD)
6. Unable to play MP4 (H.264) files in Gutsy but had no trouble in Feisty only with Totem. Works fine with VLC.- not solved, not sure where to get the restricted drivers

Other than that, it went all right. Took about two hours on wireless, although it seems to have broken a few things. One thing I wish they did during the upgrade is give an idea what some of the replacements were (like why are you replacing this file) since it's a bit hard to look stuff up while upgrading...

---

### Post by rajeevbhatta on 2007-10-26
While The distro was in its beta I upgraded using the CLI. but as soon as 18th of October I had a fresh install done. I was very impressed with the whole process. ### Post by PAFaieta on 2007-10-26
Well.. The install was ok.

however.. having been freshly victimized by the problems brought about by removing windows vista, i can't say it went flawlessly. I have a Multi-boot system and had some issues. The Boot Loader from vista stayed (common problem, apparently) so had to take care of that, and also, had to use GParted from the LiveCD of gutsy to edit the flags on my other partition (Windows XP Pro) because Partition Magic flagged it as hidden and as a result, I was stuck in a loop with an error saying Autochk.exe was missing.

in reality, it wasn't, but the fact that the partition was hidden made it impossible for XP to track down and use.

The forums turned up some info, but it involved more elaborate methods using the command line editing GRUB directly. The method using GParted worked the best. Also, since it's a GUI it's much more suited for those (like me) just entering the world of Ubuntu.

the OS itself is great, and I'm happy with it.

### Post by mgmiller on 2007-10-26
I have now updated 3 systems from Feisty to Gutsy.

The total time to download and install on each of these 3 machines was about 15 mins to download plus about an hour to do the upgrade.
Before doing any of the upgrades, I always check to see that the system is up to date and then go into Synaptic and let it test all the world wide repositories to select the best one at that moment in time.  
In synaptic, Click on Settings > Repositories and on the Ubuntu Software Tab, click the arrow next to the download From: line and select "other" on the next screen, click on Select Best Server and wait for it to finish, it runs through about 152 of them.  It will then present you with the best one at that moment in time, just select it and then, back in the main synaptic window hit the reload button.  Then close out of synaptic and do the upgrade as you normally would from the Update Manager.

I am in the US, and usually I use a USA server, but for one of the machines, it found a server in Canada was faster at that moment in time, so I let it go there.

It will disable all your third party repositories before doing the upgrade and I discovered the medibuntu repo has changed.  You can get all the info on it you need, here:
[http://www.medibuntu.org/]("http://www.medibuntu.org/")

Just click on the Repositories How To link for the instructions.


In the first (AMD xp single core 2500+ 750 MB ram ATI 9600 connected to a 37" vizio LCD via VGA), it was originally installed as 6.06, and has been upgraded as the new releases came out.  

This last upgrade actually went flawlessly, but I didn't realize it till I wasted an hour tinkering with xorg trying to get my  monitor to not shift the picture 2 inches to the left.  
I finally hit the "auto adjust" button on the monitor, and it snapped back to the right spot.  No problems since.  :lolflag:

The second machine is an AMD 64 3500+ with 2 gig ram and AGP 8x XFX brand Geforce 7800 GT connected to a Viewsonic 22" LCD via DVI-D.  

This machine was originally installed as 5.04 and has been upgraded A LOT.  
This upgrade was relatively painless, except for a slight increase in the time it now takes to display the desktop after logging in.  (45 seconds from login to desktop).  

The other problem I have is printing to a Canon i960 using the turboprint driver.  It produces great photos, but takes several minutes to get a 4x6 print out of the print que, when under Feisty, it only took a few seconds.  

I have an active bug open on it. 
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/bugs/157044]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/bugs/157044")

As this machine is also my house file server, I found it had reset all the networking shares to do not allow and so I had to get them set up again.   It's possible I did that to myself during the upgrade when it asked to update several configuration files and I said yes, I suspect one of them was all my samba settings, etc.  There is a really nice gui for setting up samba that made fixing this a snap.

```
sudo apt-get install system-config-samba
```

The third system is my sons AMD 64 dual core 5600+ with 2 gig of ram and integrated nvidia 7025 graphics running a 19" LCD via DVI-D.

He is a Linux noobie and I told him what to do over the phone and just let him go at it.  

The only problem he had was a web cam that was previously not detected in Feisty  is now detected and installed as /dev/video0 and his onboard TV tuner has become /dev/video1.  This made tvtime unusable unless you unplug the video cam and reboot.  Since he almost never uses the web cam, he doesn't really care.

On the other hand, he now has a working accelerated driver for his nvidia 7025 and he is loving compiz-fusion.  :guitar:

Anyway, that's been my experience with 3 machines, on the whole pretty good.
:popcorn:

---

### Post by Chip on 2007-10-26
Dell Inspiron 9400 Ubuntu "Gutsy" 7.10 Install -

This is also my Windows machine so I just bought another disk drive and started from scratch with the Desktop CD. Although the machine has wi-fi I have a hard cable so don't know if wi-fi works or not. The basic install went OK or at least good enough that I got something that worked to the point I could deal with it. I only had a couple of problems to deal with:

- Doesn't run the Compiz Fusion - The machine has builtin ATI Mobility Radeon X1400 controller and this seems to be disabled on purpose. Bummer. If I'm wrong on this somebody say something.

- The resolution seemed to only be 1600x1200. Period. No other choices. The machine is using the "fglxr" proprietary driver (non-restricted comes up VESA. Ugh). The screen on this machine is 1920x1200. I paid a lot for those pixels. I wanted them. The GUI utilities said that the monitor selected was "Plug 'n' Play". Other things wouldn't select. In /etc/X11/xorg.conf there was only one Modes setting and that was "1600x1200". I simply changed that to "1920x1200" and all of a sudden I had lots of choices up to and including the desired "1920x1200". I'm not sure why that simple edit opened up all these other choices. I feel I just got lucky. Perhaps somebody can enlighten me.

- I have a Logitech "Bluetooth Travel Mouse" model M-RBB93 that I like to use with the laptop. The Bluetooth adapter on the laptop seemed to come up OK but it just didn't work. I tried a number of things listed on the forums but I think they are more confusing than helpful. I found the documentation:
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/BluetoothMouse](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/BluetoothMouse)
and it worked great, including surviving reboots and power cycles on the mouse. Basically you need to make sure HIDD_ENABLED=1 in /etc/default/bluetooth. I do wonder why this isn't set this way by default if a bluetooth interface is found and enabled.

### Post by jamouneau on 2007-10-27
My impression of 7.10 is definitely mixed to unsatisfactory.  Except for a brief tryout of warty(?) this is my first ubuntu install.

Troubleshooting has not been painless.  The installation all works except for unacceptably slow internet browsing. I've attempted every "fix" proposed on the neworking forum to no avail.  The teaser is that the only website to  work, Mozilla, is blazing fast.  At least for my installation dns does not appear to be an issue.  Entering either ip or domain name results in almost instantaneous connection, followed by prolonged "waiting for google.com.." etc.

The bottom line for my experience is that I've unsuccesfully messed around with 7.10 longer than it took me to do a full OpenBSD/KDE install. One that you could actually browse with.

### Post by uitra on 2007-10-27
Done two clean installations. one update.

Non of the installations went smothe.

The upgraded broke the system, with a little help from myself. (/boot a 134 MB partition was not able to make rom for the new kernel, so the update quited, so i tyred to free up some space by running some apt-get clean something command, and then when that did not work to delete some files)

On the desktop I got some complaints about "120 00921 hda I/O buffer waiting for DMA Logic 1" many lines similar to that one. But after the install the system works, even the nvidia driver.

I took the ubuntu 7.10 to a friend to spread some ubuntu! and that was rather embarrassing.  Some minutes with this buffer I/O someting messages pupping up. Then the install goes fine. But NVIDIA drivers wont WORK. I tried reinstalling both the nvidia-glx and nvidia-glx-new. I also tried for amd and 386 versjon.

Also on my first installation on my friends computer support for ntfs did not auto mount. Had to run force mount. This was a clean install. 30 min later i made a new clean install this time the ntfs worked. Same hardware, same software, different results (before the second install I had disabled a FD that had made some of these i/o buffer complaints)

I think 7.10 never should have been released in this form to the public, now my friend will never try linux again.

Ubuntu is no windows killer, it is a system killer if your are no expert

But it does look sexy and I dont give up.. I use ubuntu with cedega, no need anymore for XP.

### Post by ian dobson on 2007-10-27
Hi,

Just upgraded my main server to 7.10, most things went well, but I had to manually fix afew things:-

1) My RAID5 array didn't come up until I removed/reinstalled mdadm with APT
2) I had to manually blacklist the intel_agp module again
3) I had to rerun lmsensors-detect again
4) I had to recompile the email server I'm using (xmail) which I manually installed anyway as apt couldn't install it.
5) Several startup scripts in rc3.d just vanished after the upgrade.

All in all no major problems apart from the blacklisting thing as I had to open up the box ând remove ram. Hardware:-

1) ASUS p5b-plus vista edition (don't ask)
2) Intel Q6600
3) 4Gb ram
4) 80Gb boot on jmicro controller
5) 4x500Gb hd's on the intel ICH8R
6) Onboard Attansic Technology Corp. L1 Gigabit Ethernet Adapter

Regards
Ian Dobson

### Post by Fruhwirth on 2007-10-27
I guess this is the appropriate place to vent :mad:.

Gutsy has been awful for me and poking around a bit, I don't see any performance or feature improvements that really help me (except that Flash is installed out of the box, which I appreciate). 

1. I had problems with my ATI Xpress Radeon 200m graphics card and hardware acceleration.  I got that to work in about a couple days - read about that [here.]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=585172") Dapper has problems with this same issue, so that's not really Gutsy's fault in particular, just a lingering annoyance that *still* hasn't been fixed.  The restricted driver installer in Gutsy was nice and did help, but not really that much. 

2. I still have problems with my RaLink RT2500 802.11g Cardbus/mini-PCI (rev 01). Almost 10 days since I installed Gutsy and I still don't have wireless.  Been trying to follow[ this HOWTO]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=563547"), but with no luck.  

3. And because I've spent all my time on the above problems, I haven't even begun to try to fix a big boot problem I'm having. On boot, right after it says it's loading GRUB, the screen goes black for 4-5 minutes, then the login screen finally comes on. Because I haven't begun to research it, I don't know how to check error logs, etc., for this problem.  But it makes troubleshooting all other problems very annoying since each restart is such a huge time commitment.

I'm probably going to take the day and reinstall Dapper. That's my experience with Gutsy.

I loved Ubuntu. After 9 days with Gutsy, I'm thinking of calling off the wedding :(

UPDATE!!!

I did a "fresher" install (format install partition *and* delete all hidden data files from home partition) and reinstalled Gutsy.  Problem #1 above was no longer and issue. The driver installed correctly out the box without tinkering which is actually really impressive. Problem #2 above was not a problem either!  Problem #3 above seemed like a potentially very icky driver or kernel problem, but it was solved quickly with[ this]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=3754581#post3754581"). Lovely. 

Despite initial rage, frustration and skepticism, I am now very impressed with Gutsy.

### Post by markwren on 2007-10-27
Having tried to upgrade Ubuntu at every previous release and failed (ending up installing from scratch each time) I was very surprised when it went smoothly and without a hitch.

Especially considering I run a laptop (and always have since first using Linux 10 years ago).

Networking needs restarting after boot so as to get the Atheros WiFi working properly - but then it always did on previous versions.

Even VMWare 5.5 ran after applying the very latest vmware-any-any patch prior to recompiling the kernel modules.

And the SATA HDD kernel drivers seem to perform better now.

I like compiz, and I've added some extra elements through the manager. It works very well except for the workspace manager losing some minor functionality (cannot drag & drop windows from within the applet); and Google Earth has become unstable - it cannot do internal panel/window management properly under compiz - the sky or map view blanks when the menu is used, pop-up panels only part show, etc. 

All round verdict: Great work .......

### Post by Raptor Ramjet on 2007-10-27
Well I'm afraid I've had a bit of a nightmare with the upgrade from Feisty to Gutsy.  After initially upgrading I simply couldn't get my monitor recognised properly and sound was only working intermiitently.

So... After much faffing about with the new GUI based Xorg config tools I thought I might as well start again with a "clean slate" so I did a complete reinstall.  Before performing the installation I also removed all my "." files ("dotfiles") and when installing I formatted my "/" partition and (my "/home" directory nbeing on its own partition).  Once the install had finished I then spent a good hour adding NVIDIA drivers mp3 support  etc. etc.   After this I thought I'd install the real time kernel as I'd really like to use the machine to do some audio work.

Oh dear.  After rebooting X was broken with messages about incompatible kernel headers and despite much use of apt to remove and reinstall stuff plus a number of attempts at using "dpkg-reconfigure xserver-org" etc. I just couldn't get X working again.

So... I reinstalled again (again formatting "/") after which sound was still giving me problems - I have a motherboard with onboard sound and an SBLIve card and it was random chance which soundcard was assigned to card(0) on bootup.   As I only wanted to use the SBLIve I got round this problem by disabling the onboard sound in my BIOS.

However my biggest problem is that Xorg is absolutely refusing to drive my monitor at the correct resolution and will not use anything better than 1280x1024 @ 52hz which, to be frank, looks really crap (my monitors optimal settings are 1280x1024 @ 85hz)

And this is despite me manually editing xorg.conf to add in new "Monitor" and "Screen" sections that were copied from my old xorg.conf (I backed up "/etc" before upgrading).  I've even tried adding all the required "modeline" entries as detected by Knoppix but to no avail.

So all in all I'm not entirely happy with the Gutsy update.  There's a lot of good stuff but my Xorg problems mean that it's a pain to use the machine for any length of time as the monitor setup makes my eyes tire very quickly (a refresh rate of 52Hz is crap !)  If I don't get it working well in the next few days I'm afraid I'll have to revert to Feisty as my monitor worked perfectly under it.

Sorry.

### Post by zyg0t3 on 2007-10-27
The biggest issue i had with Gutsy was wifi setup. It was basically a no-go in 64bit. Restricted broadcom firmware didn't work for me and i couldn't, after searching for 2 days, find any proper drivers for ndiswrapper.
 
So i wiped the partition, installed 32bit gutsy and got ndiswrapper working. 

Other than that, i had problems with blackscreens at boot up with no usplash. Corrected that by putting in vga=491 to the boot line in /boot/grub/menu.lst
